topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee was scalding, mirroring the heat rising in my chest. I stared at the faded photograph on the table—us, grinning like idiots under a ridiculously bright summer sun. Liam, my oldest confidant, the keeper of all my embarrassing secrets, was supposed to be here. He was late. Twenty minutes late. When he finally shambled in, looking perfectly content, he clapped me on the shoulder with that easy, familiar gesture. "Hey, what's up?" he asked, his eyes bright and unfocused. I swallowed, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult. "Nothing," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally flat. I watched him settle into the armchair, completely oblivious to the tectonic shift happening inside me. Later, when the conversation drifted to old times, I tried to bring up that disastrous camping trip from seventh grade. "Remember when we tried to build that fire?" I asked, a sharp edge creeping into my tone. Liam just blinked slowly. "Huh? No, I don't think so. What are you talking about?" The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing down on my lungs. I felt a furious tightening behind my eyes, a desperate need for him to *see* it, to acknowledge the shared history that was suddenly dissolving into static.

[story 2]

I slammed the door shut, the sound echoing in the small apartment like a gunshot. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the counter to steady myself. Liam had promised. He had *promised* he would remember the summer we spent trying to learn to surf, the ridiculous sunburns, the whispered dreams under the moonlight. Now, he looked at me with that placid, distant expression, as if I were some stranger who had wandered into his living room. "It was a good summer," I said, the words tasting like ash. My jaw was clenched so tight I could feel the strain in my molars. I watched his mouth move, searching for a flicker of recognition, a shared memory, anything. Nothing. Just a blank slate. A wave of hot, frustrated energy washed over me, making my skin prickle. I took a deep, ragged breath, trying to force the tightness out of my chest, but it only made the pressure worse. "You don't remember anything," I stated, the accusation hanging heavy between us, sharp and undeniable. His brow furrowed slightly, a flicker of confusion crossing his face, but it wasn't the recognition I craved. It was just... unfamiliarity.

[story 3]

The silence stretched between us, taut and brittle. I paced the worn rug, the rhythm of my steps a frantic counterpoint to the stillness of the room. Every instinct screamed at me to shout, to demand an explanation, to tear the reality apart until the truth of my forgotten history surfaced. I stopped abruptly, turning to face him, my posture rigid, every muscle coiled with suppressed energy. "We used to talk about everything," I articulated, my voice low, vibrating with a controlled intensity that bordered on dangerous. I watched him process the words, his gaze drifting around the room, seemingly searching for an anchor that wasn't there. He offered a weak, apologetic smile, the kind you give someone who genuinely doesn't grasp the gravity of the situation. That casual dismissal struck me like a physical blow. My knuckles were white where I gripped my own arms. I felt a deep, burning resentment bubble up, a raw, ugly feeling that wanted to boil over. I wanted him to feel the weight of what he was erasing, the sheer, monumental effort required to simply not recall a bond that felt as solid as bedrock.
